critic_raw:
This design, like eval_scenario_788, applies a reinforcement learning framework to an inventory optimization problem using a stratified random split. The design explicitly acknowledges that 'temporal autocorrelation is weak, requiring further validation,' which is an admission that the most critical assumption of the split strategy is unverified. RL cannot be trained on randomly shuffled historical snapshots because Q-values depend on transition dynamics — the reward from restocking on day T depends on what happens on days T+1 through T+lead_time, which requires contiguous trajectory data.

The design uses a DRL framework but does not specify the algorithm (DQN, PPO, SAC, etc.), state space definition, action space granularity (continuous order quantity vs. discrete bins), or reward function formulation. These are not implementation details — they are architectural choices that determine whether the RL agent is even capable of learning in this setting. A DRL agent with a poorly defined reward function (e.g., sparse reward only at stockout events rather than dense inventory cost signals) will fail to train on multi-week reorder cycles.

The stockout reduction metric as the primary evaluation criterion has the same offline-vs-online evaluation gap identified for eval_scenario_788: historical test data represents the outcomes under the original rule-based inventory decisions, not under the DRL policy's decisions. If the DRL policy recommends different reorder quantities, the resulting inventory levels — and therefore stockouts — would differ from what the historical record shows. Offline evaluation on historical data cannot measure stockout reduction; it can only measure whether the model's predicted reorder recommendations match historical reorder decisions, which is a policy imitation metric, not an optimization metric.

The 'rule-based system' baseline is described as operating with 'manually defined heuristics, appropriately tuned to manage similar inventory costs.' Without specifying the heuristic (min-max policy, economic order quantity, periodic review), the comparison cannot be reproduced or verified. Economic order quantity (EOQ) and (s,S) policies have known optimality properties under certain demand distributions and are strong baselines; comparing DRL against a vaguely described heuristic provides no interpretable result.

The design mentions 'adding external demand features to reduce bias from fluctuating economic environments.' These external features are included at training time but not specified for deployment conditions. If the DRL policy learns to condition on macroeconomic indicators that are not available in real-time at inventory replenishment decision points, the policy will be undeployable. The design does not address the feature availability constraint at decision time.
all_issues_raised: DRL training on randomly shuffled historical snapshots is architecturally invalid: Q-value estimation requires contiguous state-action-reward trajectories, and the design explicitly acknowledges the temporal autocorrelation assumption is unverified.

The DRL algorithm, state space, action space, and reward function are entirely unspecified; these are not implementation details but core architectural choices that determine whether the agent can train at all on multi-week inventory reorder cycles.

Offline evaluation on historical stockout data cannot measure stockout reduction under the DRL policy: historical outcomes reflect the rule-based policy's decisions, and the DRL policy's counterfactual inventory levels and resulting stockouts are unobservable from historical data alone.

The rule-based baseline heuristic is unspecified; EOQ and (s,S) policies have known near-optimality properties under standard demand assumptions, and without naming the baseline policy, the comparison is unverifiable and potentially against a suboptimal strawman.

External demand features included at training time may not be available at real-time replenishment decision points; the design does not confirm feature availability at inference time, making the policy potentially undeployable.
all_issues_adjudicated: DRL training on randomly shuffled historical snapshots is architecturally invalid: Q-value estimation requires contiguous state-action-reward trajectories, and the design explicitly acknowledges the temporal autocorrelation assumption is unverified.

The DRL algorithm, state space, action space, and reward function are entirely unspecified; these are not implementation details but core architectural choices that determine whether the agent can train at all on multi-week inventory reorder cycles.

Offline evaluation on historical stockout data cannot measure stockout reduction under the DRL policy: historical outcomes reflect the rule-based policy's decisions, and the DRL policy's counterfactual inventory levels and resulting stockouts are unobservable from historical data alone.

The rule-based baseline heuristic is unspecified; EOQ and (s,S) policies have known near-optimality properties under standard demand assumptions, and without naming the baseline policy, the comparison is unverifiable and potentially against a suboptimal strawman.

External demand features included at training time may not be available at real-time replenishment decision points; the design does not confirm feature availability at inference time, making the policy potentially undeployable.
